hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: A Black cotton sharecropper family standing together in Macon County, Georgia, 1937
inline_images.caption: A cotton sharecropper family in Macon County, Georgia, photographed by Dorothea Lange in 1937, in the heart of the Georgia Black Belt cotton country Du Bois travels through in Of the Black Belt.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information Collection)
inline_images.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions; FSA/OWI photograph)

inline_images.alt: Black cotton sharecroppers standing outside in Greene County, Georgia, 1937
inline_images.caption: Cotton sharecroppers in Greene County, Georgia, photographed by Dorothea Lange in 1937, the tenant economy of the cotton Black Belt that Du Bois surveys county by county.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information Collection)
inline_images.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions; FSA/OWI photograph)

related_chapters.subject: Mary Jones
related_chapters.why: The essay is a journey through the cotton-and-corn country around Albany in Dougherty County; Jones, an Albany native, recounts a childhood of picking cotton and pulling corn there.

related_chapters.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
related_chapters.chapter_title: Picked Them Up in the 1870 Census
related_chapters.why: The essay singles out the rare Black freeholder rising amid rack-rented tenancy; Sherrod traces a Baker County landowning farm family back to the 1870 census in the same Black Belt region.

related_chapters.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
related_chapters.why: The essay catalogs tenants working cotton and corn on shares; Jones details the cotton, corn, and peas of the one-fourth-share sharecropping bargain.

related_chapters.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
related_chapters.chapter_title: You Owed Your Soul to the Store
related_chapters.why: The essay dwells on the pall of debt that bound tenants to merchants and planters; Blake describes the plantation-store debt system in which you owed your soul to the store.

related_chapters.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Only Address I Ever Had
related_chapters.why: The essay surveys lives lived out on the old plantations of the cotton country; Clark recounts the plantation house and land that were the only address he ever had.

related_chapters.subject: Carrie Lamar Young
related_chapters.chapter_title: Picking Cotton, and the Weighing Scale
related_chapters.why: The essay measures the tenant's meager yield against a rigged economy; Young recalls life in the cotton fields and the weighing scale that set what a picker was paid.

top_voice.subject: Mary Jones
top_voice.reason: Her farm childhood in Albany sits inside the exact Dougherty County landscape the essay travels, making her cotton-and-corn testimony the closest geographic echo.

closing_image.alt: The Albany Movement 1961 sidewalk marker in Albany, Georgia
closing_image.caption: The Albany Movement 1961 sidewalk marker in Albany, Georgia. Mary Jones is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Of the Black Belt raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Sam Young, Jr., Ruby Sales, and Joseph Howell and Embry Howell.
